planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; leopard plant is one of the finest shade perennials for the Gulf Coast, tolerating both deep shade and the summer heat and humidity that defeat many shade perennials in zone 8–9.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade — leopard plant is one of the most valuable and underused shade perennials for the Gulf Coast, producing extraordinarily bold, round, glossy, deep-green, leathery leaves up to 18 inches across that retain a tropical freshness through the hottest Gulf Coast summers. The common cultivar 'Aureomaculatum' (spotted leopard plant) has large yellow spots scattered across the dark green leaves, giving a striking, almost artificial-looking pattern. Yellow daisy flowers appear in fall to winter on tall stalks, providing late-season color when most shade perennials are shutting down. Completely evergreen in zone 8–10; the leathery leaves resist frost damage better than most shade perennials and the plant is reliably root-hardy through zone 7 winters. Clumps increase slowly and need division only every 5–7 years. An outstanding long-term, zero-maintenance shade perennial for Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; fragrant hosta is more heat tolerant than most hostas and is one of the best hosta species for zone 8–9, where it performs more reliably than the northern-bred cultivars that need cold winters to thrive.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to dappled shade — fragrant hosta stands apart from all other hostas by being the only species with intensely, beautifully fragrant flowers — large, pure white, trumpet-shaped blooms on tall scapes in August that fill the evening garden with a sweet, lily-like perfume. The light-green, glossy, somewhat corrugated leaves are unlike the typical blue-green, waxy hostas — they are brighter and more luminous in the shade garden. Fragrant hosta is also significantly more heat-tolerant than most hosta cultivars, performing reliably in zone 8–9 where most hybrid hostas struggle to thrive without cold winters. The evening fragrance is outstanding when planted near patios, pathways, and windows. Derived cultivars like 'Royal Standard' and 'Aphrodite' (double flowers) extend the bloom period and flower character. Exceptional as a long-lived, low-maintenance shade perennial for warm-climate gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; Siebold's hosta is one of the larger-leafed hostas — allow generous spacing as clumps can eventually reach 4 feet across.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade — Siebold's hosta is one of the most majestic and widely grown of all hosta species, producing enormous, boldly corrugated, glaucous blue-green to blue-gray leaves up to 18 inches long that give the shade garden a dramatic, tropical-foliage effect. The sluggish pale lavender flowers on low, barely-visible scapes in early summer are secondary to the spectacular foliage. Famous cultivars derived from this species include 'Elegans' (intensely blue, deeply corrugated), 'Frances Williams' (blue with gold-green margins), and 'Big Daddy' (deeply puckered, intensely blue). The waxy, powder-coated leaf surface repels water and resists slug damage somewhat better than thin-leaved hostas. While this species is at its finest in cool-climate zone 4–7 gardens, it grows adequately in zone 8 in deep shade with consistent moisture and limited afternoon sun exposure.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; blue hosta is notably more heat tolerant than most blue-leafed hosta cultivars and seeds relatively true — seedlings often produce good, deep-green to slightly glaucous plants.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade — blue hosta (Hosta ventricosa) is a large, vigorous species from eastern Asia notable for producing some of the deepest, most attractive purple-lavender flowers in the genus — tall, attractive scapes of deep violet bell-shaped flowers in midsummer that are significantly more ornamental than the pale flowers of most hostas. The large, rounded, dark green to slightly glaucous leaves form a lush, mounded clump 2–3 feet tall and 3–4 feet wide. It is also one of the more heat-tolerant hosta species, performing well in zone 8–9 in consistently moist, deeply shaded conditions where many hybrid cultivars decline. Unlike most hostas, it seeds true from open-pollinated seeds, making it easy to naturalize in appropriate shaded conditions. The cultivar 'Aureomarginata' (golden-edged blue hosta) adds variegated interest with a gold to cream margin on the dark leaves.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy, moderately dry to moist soil in full sun to partial shade; narrowleaf bluestar is slow to establish from seed but extremely long-lived once settled — the fine-textured foliage and exceptional fall color are among the finest in the native perennial garden.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately dry to moist soil in full sun to partial shade — narrowleaf bluestar from Arkansas is widely considered to have the finest fall color of any perennial plant in North America, its masses of thread-like, willow-narrow leaves turning brilliant, pure golden-yellow in September–October and holding the color for weeks, creating a soft, misty cloud of gold that is breathtaking in mass plantings. The spring bloom of pale blue, star-shaped flowers in May is also very attractive, and the fine, feathery summer texture contrasts beautifully with bold-leaved companions. It is exceptionally drought tolerant once established with a deep root system, heat tolerant to zone 9, and completely deer-resistant. The Perennial Plant Association named it Perennial Plant of the Year in 2011. A near-perfect low-maintenance native perennial — plant it in full sun to partial shade and essentially never tend to it again.

planting.note: Root plantlets (spiderettes) by pinning them to moist soil or placing in water; in zone 9b–11, spider plant can be grown as an outdoor groundcover in deep shade where its stolons will root and spread to cover ground. In zone 8–9a, treat as a container plant or protect from frost.

directions: Root spiderette plantlets in water or moist soil and plant in any moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade — spider plant is one of the world's most popular houseplants and a valuable outdoor shade groundcover in tropical and subtropical gardens, producing fountains of long, arching, strap-like leaves (solid green or with bold cream-white centers or margins) and long, arching stolons tipped with miniature plantlets that root wherever they contact moist soil. In zone 9b–11 it grows as a naturalized groundcover in deep shade under trees, producing a lush carpet that requires almost no care. It tolerates deep shade, inconsistent watering, and low soil fertility better than almost any other shade groundcover. The variegated form (cream-and-green striped) is especially bright in dark shade. NASA studies identified spider plant as one of the most effective air-purifying houseplants, removing formaldehyde and other volatile organic compounds from indoor air.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade; Formosa toad lily is more heat tolerant than the Japanese toad lily (T. hirta) and is the toad lily species to choose for zone 7–9 gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade — Formosa toad lily is a shade garden gem from Taiwan, producing extraordinarily intricate, orchid-like flowers in September–November with white petals heavily spotted with purple-maroon and a bright yellow central structure, on arching stems with glossy, clasping leaves. Unlike most flowering plants, it blooms in the cooling days of fall precisely when the shade garden most needs color. It is more heat tolerant than the Japanese toad lily (T. hirta), making it the better species for zone 7–9 gardens where summer heat can stress T. hirta. The stolon-spreading habit allows it to naturalize over several years into expanding colonies in appropriate moist, shaded woodland conditions. The botanical intricacy of the individual flowers rewards close inspection — few shade perennials produce a more beautifully detailed flower. Outstanding in shaded borders alongside hostas, ferns, and autumn fern.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container transplants in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bog sage spreads aggressively by stolons in ideal moist conditions and requires regular division or containment to prevent it from overwhelming smaller garden neighbors.

directions: Root cuttings and plant in moist to consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — bog sage from South America produces one of the most pure, clear, vivid sky-blue flower colors available in any garden plant: small, two-lipped, brilliant cerulean-blue flowers in dense terminal spikes from July through hard frost, on tall, gracefully arching stems of narrow, bright green leaves with a pleasant herbal fragrance. It grows naturally in boggy, wet meadows and stream margins and requires consistently moist soil — ideal for rain gardens, wet low areas, and the moist margins of ponds. The plant spreads vigorously by underground stolons in suitable conditions, eventually forming large patches: excellent for covering wet, difficult areas but needs management in smaller beds. In zone 7–8 it may die back in winter but resprouts reliably from established stolons; in zone 9–10 it grows as a large, nearly evergreen perennial. One of the finest late-season blue flowers for the moist garden.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; loblolly bay requires strongly acidic, consistently moist to wet soil — it will not grow in neutral, alkaline, or dry conditions and must have peaty, humus-rich, well-aerated acid soil.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich, peaty, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun — loblolly bay is a spectacular native evergreen tree of the Gulf Coast and Atlantic coastal plain bay swamps and wet pine flatwoods, producing 2.5–3 inch, pure white, camellia-like flowers with prominent gold stamens that appear from June through October in the hottest part of the summer when few other flowering trees bloom. The dark, glossy, evergreen leaves are handsome year-round; the bark is attractively furrowed and gray. It grows naturally in the acidic, peaty, frequently saturated soils of bay swamps, seeps, and wet flatwood depressions — conditions exactly matching an acidic, moist bog garden or the wet margins of a natural pond. It is closely related to the camellia family and equally demanding in its soil pH requirements; failure is almost always caused by insufficiently acidic soil. An outstanding native flowering tree for wet, acidic, shaded garden sites in zone 7–9.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to light shade; Texas larkspur is a cool-season annual or biennial that requires cold stratification — fall sowing in Gulf Coast gardens provides natural stratification and leads to bloom in April–May before summer heat arrives.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — Texas larkspur is the native delphinium of southern prairies, limestone cedar glades, and rocky roadsides from Texas to the Carolinas, producing tall, slender spikes of deeply blue, white, or blue-and-white bicolored flowers in April–May before summer heat arrives, with the delicate, finely cut, ferny foliage typical of the delphinium family. The flowers are intensely attractive to ruby-throated hummingbirds and long-tongued native bees. Unlike the Pacific Giant and other hybrid garden delphiniums that struggle in heat and humidity, Texas larkspur is fully adapted to the alkaline limestone soils, hot springs, and dry summers of the southern prairies. It self-sows readily to maintain naturalized colonies in open, well-drained, sunny conditions and is excellent for wildflower meadow plantings in the southern Gulf Coast and Texas Hill Country. All parts are toxic if consumed.
